key_insight: Tuning the geometry of a void inside each grain can create elastic anisotropy and shear softening, cutting the apparent shear modulus of dense ordered packings by up to 90% without requiring auxetic grains.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a minimal geometry-based design route for soft granular metamaterials whose bulk stiffness can be targeted through grain shape rather than material composition.
method: Finite element simulations of dense, ordered granular packings
Constant solid-fraction shear loading with embedded-void grain geometries
Comparison of packing-scale modulus against contact-force anisotropy, system size, and friction
evidence_signal: The abstract reports up to a 90% reduction in apparent shear modulus, with the trend correlating well with reduced contact-force anisotropy and remaining insensitive to system size and friction variation.
watch_item: The result is demonstrated in dense ordered packings under controlled simulation conditions, so translation to disordered or experimentally imperfect packings may require additional validation.
paper_title: Geometry-mediated shear softening in dense ordered granular packings
